figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dispersion of passive scalar in parallel flow with c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2). Left: velocity profile u(y); right: time history of the cross-sectional mean c̄(x, t).
FIG. 2. Contours of c(x, y, t) and slices of c̄(x, t) with c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2).

FIG. 3. Convergence of the analytical model family for the problem in Eq. (1) with increasing number of equations, measured by root-mean-square error (RMSE) over t ∈ [0, 2] with initial condition c̄0(x).
TABLE I. Key parameters used in Secs. III and IV.
FIG. 4. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for spectral-based reduced models. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 15 and nymode = n.
FIG. 5. Time histories of c̄ (left) and the error in c̄ (right) for the 3-equation reduced model obtained from direct-fit.
FIG. 6. Time histories of c̄ (left) and the error in c̄ (right) for the 3-equation reduced model obtained from the analytical closure.
FIG. 7. Time histories of c̄ (left) and the error in c̄ (right) for the 3-equation reduced model obtained from eGFM, with nxmode = 15 and nymode = 3.
FIG. 8. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 6. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.
FIG. 9. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 7, . . . , 10. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.

FIG. 10. Dispersion of passive scalar in the complex asymmetric parallel flow in Eq. (78) for the test case with c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2). Left: velocity profile u(y); right: time history of the crosssectional mean c̄(x, t).
FIG. 11. Contours of c(x, y, t) and slices of c̄(x, t) for the complex asymmetric parallel flow with initial condition c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2).
FIG. 12. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for spectral-based reduced models in the complex asymmetric flow. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 15 and nymode = n.

FIG. 13. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for velocity-based reduced models in the complex asymmetric flow. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 15 and nymode = n. For n ≥ 6, no successful models were obtained and these cases are omitted.
FIG. 14. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 6 in the complex asymmetric flow. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.
FIG. 15. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 7, . . . , 10 in the complex asymmetric flow. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.

FIG. 16. RMSE of eGFM velocity-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 5 in the complex asymmetric flow. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup. For n ≥ 6, no successful models were obtained and these cases are omitted.
FIG. 17. Passive scalar dispersion in inhomogeneous flow I, Eq. (80), with initial condition c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2). Left: streamline plot; right: time history of the cross-sectional mean c̄(x, t).
FIG. 18. Passive scalar dispersion in inhomogeneous flow II, Eq. (81), with initial condition c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2). Left: streamline plot; right: time history of the cross-sectional mean c̄(x, t).
FIG. 19. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for spectral-based reduced models in inhomogeneous flow I. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 20 and nymode = n. Only successful models are shown.

FIG. 20. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for velocity-based reduced models in inhomogeneous flow I. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 20 and nymode = n. Only successful direct-fit models are shown.
FIG. 21. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 5 in inhomogeneous flow I. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.

FIG. 22. RMSE of eGFM velocity-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 4 in inhomogeneous flow I. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup. The n = 5 case is omitted because no successful model is obtained.

FIG. 23. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for spectral-based reduced models in inhomogeneous flow II. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 20 and nymode = n. Only successful direct-fit models are shown.

FIG. 24. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for velocity-based reduced models in inhomogeneous flow II. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 20 and nymode = n. Only successful direct-fit models are shown.
FIG. 25. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 5 in inhomogeneous flow II. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.

FIG. 26. RMSE of eGFM velocity-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, 2, 3 in inhomogeneous flow II. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup. The n ≥ 4 cases are omitted because essentially no successful model is obtained.
FIG. 27. Passive scalar dispersion for the problem in Eq. (87) with initial condition c̄0(x) = exp(−40x2), ensemble-averaged over 10 000 realizations.
FIG. 28. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for spectral-based reduced models in the random-flow case. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 5 and nymode = n.
FIG. 29. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 6 in the random-flow case. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.
FIG. 30. RMSE of eGFM spectral-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 7, . . . , 10 in the random-flow case. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup.
FIG. 31. Comparison of the RMSE of c̄ for velocity-based reduced models in the random-flow case. For the eGFM-based models, we use nxmode = 5 and nymode = n.

FIG. 32. RMSE of eGFM velocity-based reduced models over the space of forcing subsets for n = 1, . . . , 4 in the random-flow case. Gray blocks indicate fitting failure or runtime blowup. The n = 5 case is omitted because no successful model is obtained.
